title: Sailing club faces closure after Crown Lands hikes rent by over 600 per cent
author: Declan Bowring
fullText: A Sydney community sailing club is facing closure after NSW Crown Lands increased their annual land use fee by 648 per cent.Port Hacking Open Sailing Club in Sydney's Sutherland Shire usually pays a $660 permissive occupancy fee to the state government because its facility is based on crown land.However, the volunteer-run club was recently notified that the fee would increase to $9,908.After a discount was applied due to their status as a non-profit community sport club, the fee was adjusted to $4,958.Commodore Scott Roberts from the club said the increased fee, which was due in April, would be too much for the club.Scott Roberts says the rent hike will force the club to close. (Supplied: Port Hacking Open Sailing Club)"We can't operate with that because as the fees go up for membership, membership obviously declines because you're in competition with so many other sports," Mr Roberts said."It's a real nail in the coffin for the sailing club when the government thinks that we should be paying a lot."But 100 per cent of our focus is putting back in the community — and the land is not locked up for use by private individuals or wealthy individuals. It's open to the public."The club consists of a pair of boat sheds and a small clubhouse, and does not have a restaurant or gaming facilities like some larger NSW clubs.Mr Roberts said the not-for-profit club earned around $30,000 a year, which was all spent due to the club's non-profit status.He said the club was mostly funded by annual membership fees, which cost around $8,500.More NSW clubs face rent hikePort Hacking Open Sailing Club is not the only club to have faced a recent rent hike by Crown Lands.The volunteer-run Port Kembla Sailing Club on the state's south coast had their annual rent increased from $535 to $973 in 2023.Initially, Crown Lands had invoiced for $1,700, representing a 300 per cent increase, before waiving the fee down."I got in touch with Crown Lands and I explained the story, I said we can't afford it," Port Kembla Sailing Club president Stephen Watts said."They said that they were going to review it in September 2026, which is coming up."Bondi Bowling Club in Sydney's eastern suburbs also faced a 300 per cent rent increase in 2023, after Crown Lands said it had assessed its rent based on "market value".Case-by-case applicationNSW Premier Chris Minns said Crown Lands took a case-by-case approach to reducing rent as much as possible for non-profit clubs."We're not trying to gouge people," Mr Minns told 702 ABC Sydney today."That's waterfront land. I mean, if it was commercial rates it wouldn't be $5,000 a year — that's less than $100 a week."I do know that the [Crown Lands Minister Steve Kamper] has looked at it on a case-by-case basis and said if we don't want this organisation to go, we want it to continue to operate, so maybe I can take it on board and have a look at it."Mr Minns said the rent was put back into Crown Lands, which he described as a sometimes "undercapitalised" part of the state's asset base."We don't use it for consolidated revenue, we don't use it to prop up politicians' pay," he said.702 ABC Sydney has contacted Mr Kamper's office for comment.NSW member for Cronulla Mark Speakman, whose electorate covered Port Hacking, said going after clubs was a sign the Labor government had "lost control of its budget"."It's resorting to gouging community groups as one way to help clean up its budget mess," he said.
summary: The small volunteer-run club in Sydney's Sutherland Shire says the sudden hike will force it to close its doors.
